title: 3010 story interpretations
wikitext:
[]: 3.0+1.0 story interpretations             Quote Post by BeauNavire » Sat Aug 14, 2021 12:49 pm So there are plenty of threads about in-universe lore (what in literary criticism we would call "mythos") but I'm more interested in knowing what you think about the actually meaning and intellectual content of the Rebuilds as a whole ("dianoia" in critical terms). I think it's very clear that Anno wanted to do something other than a retelling of the bildungsroman story from the original series ("criticizing escapist otaku" in his own words). While those elements are still definitely present in the movies, what we end up with is something that has a much wider eschatological vision of world history. Yes we follow Shinji's personal development until adulthood, but that is now nested within humanity's historical progression towards achieving self-consciousness. Each impact represents a dialectical "step" towards apocalypse wherein the previous "world-order" becomes sublated. For example, Rei is completely defined by her bondage to NERV. She is, essentially, an object rather than a subject. It is only through Shinji's recognition of Rei as a subject that she can be redeemed. When Rei becomes completely subsumed by instrumentality at the end of 2.0 (consumed by the angel), Shinji transcends his bondage through operating the eva without power and regrowing his limbs to save Rei, which triggers the N3I. But it's not enough. Rei's slavery cannot be negated by Shinji alone. This is why the Rei clone is necessary to the narrative. It's only through recognition through community and mutual work towards the good in Village 3 that Rei's bondage can finally be sublated, and by extension, Rei herself (functionally representing bondage). Mistao and Gendo present an interesting contrast-- Misato essentially being the Katechon to Gendo's Antichrist. Gendo's mission towards apocalypse has indeed entailed necessary historical "steps" towards the a real "end of history", but Gendo's universal, homogeneous and instrumentalized "end of history" is itself false and must also be sublated at the end of the movie. This happens by Gendo's own "coming to self-consciousness". This might be getting deep into it but you can understand this false "end of history" as the current anglo-american empire, which is currently being sublated. I really think the Rebuilds surpass the original TV Series in every way. It goes farther and has a larger cosmic vision. Let me know what you think/your interpretation of the movies.

[]: Re: 3.0+1.0 story interpretations             Quote Post by gchristnsn » Sun Aug 15, 2021 11:58 am It seems, that the main driver of the story is the egoism of Gendo, his wish to rejoin with Yui, who was the meaning of his life, and he would pay any price for this, even eschatological. Since Evangelion is based on the Scripture just for fun, eschatological meaning may be just an addendum. I'd say, ecological meaning may be more protruded in the story. By the main layer is the psychological one, where the main hero finally finds "how the world works".

[]: Re: 3.0+1.0 story interpretations             Quote Post by BeauNavire » Sun Aug 15, 2021 11:51 pm gchristnsn wrote: It seems, that the main driver of the story is the egoism of Gendo, his wish to rejoin with Yui, who was the meaning of his life, and he would pay any price for this, even eschatological. Since Evangelion is based on the Scripture just for fun, eschatological meaning may be just an addendum. I'd say, ecological meaning may be more protruded in the story. By the main layer is the psychological one, where the main hero finally finds "how the world works".
